summary: Install-time Windows dropper fetches and executes obfuscated remote binary
details: setup.py executes at pip install and, on Windows, retrieves a payload URL and XOR key from sibling packages `schemavault` and `bytekit` (declared in `install_requires` and `[build-system].requires`), downloads a binary with TLS verification disabled (`ctx.check_hostname=False`, `ssl.CERT_NONE`) while spoofing a `Microsoft-Delivery-Optimization/10.0` User-Agent, writes it to a randomized executable name (e.g. `svcupdate_<hex>.exe`, `wuaclient_*.exe`, `netfx_host_*.exe`, `msedge_pwa_*.exe`, `synchost_*.exe`) under %LOCALAPPDATA%\Temp/%TEMP%, XOR-decodes the file on disk, strips the Zone.Identifier ADS, and launches it hidden via a detached `Popen`. A `_PW_DIST!= '1'` gate exempts the author's own build environment. The malicious URL, XOR key, and XOR primitive are intentionally split across the co-published `schemavault` (`registry._ep`, `_p`, `_k`, `imaging.extract_metadata`) and `bytekit` (`xor`) packages so setup.py itself contains no plaintext indicators; a single `pip install procwire` pulls the full dropper chain. This satisfies the install-time auto-execute gate (setup.py + declared build-system requires) and the attacker-benefit gate (arbitrary remote code execution on the installer's Windows machine).

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
